name: Licorice
summary: Licorice is a root monograph used for soothing GI/respiratory positioning and for endocrine-adjacent effects driven largely by glycyrrhizin. It is a high-leverage addition because whole-root licorice and DGL are not interchangeable from a safety standpoint.
description: Licorice root is used as tea, decoction, powder, extract, and deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL) products. Major constituents include glycyrrhizin, liquiritigenin, isoliquiritigenin, and glabridin. Frontend logic should split whole-root licorice from DGL whenever blood-pressure or potassium risk matters.
mechanism: Mechanism includes 11beta-HSD2 inhibition with downstream mineralocorticoid-like effects plus anti-inflammatory and mucosal-support activity from flavonoid constituents.
safetyNotes: Chronic or high-glycyrrhizin intake can raise blood pressure, lower potassium, and increase edema or arrhythmia risk in susceptible users.
interactions: and other drugs affected by potassium or blood-pressure shifts.
dosage: Product-specific dosing; DGL and whole-root licorice must be separated
preparation: Teas, decoctions, powders, extracts, and DGL chewables/capsules.
region: Native to southwestern Asia and parts of Europe; cultivated broadly.
